metadata.title: Portland Area Native Plants & Weeds
metadata.description: A comprehensive list of native plants and invasive weeds found in the Portland, Oregon metropolitan area, based on the City of Portland Plant List and related regional resources.

plants.notes: One of the tallest native conifers in the Pacific Northwest.
Largest maple species native to North America. Important wildlife tree.
Brilliant fall color. Often multi-stemmed and sprawling in shade.
Nitrogen-fixing tree, important for riparian restoration.
Evergreen broadleaf tree with distinctive peeling red bark.
State tree of British Columbia. Showy white bracts in spring.
Only native ash in the Pacific Northwest. Important floodplain species.
Drought-tolerant pine with distinctive vanilla-scented bark.
Leaves tremble in the slightest breeze. Spreads by root sprouts.
Largest native poplar. Fast growing riparian tree.
Oregon's state tree. Dominant conifer of the Pacific Northwest.
Only native oak in Oregon. High wildlife value. Savanna species.
Riparian tree. Good for streambank stabilization.
Adaptable willow found in a variety of habitats.
Slow-growing understory conifer. Source of taxol cancer treatment.
State tree of Washington. Tolerates deep shade.
Long-lived, rot-resistant conifer. Important cultural tree for Indigenous peoples.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries attract birds
plants.notes: Thorny native tree. Dense thickets provide excellent wildlife cover.

plants.wildlife_value: Fruit eaten by birds and mammals
plants.notes: Only native apple in the Pacific Northwest.

plants.wildlife_value: Cherries eaten by birds
plants.notes: Small native cherry. Bitter fruit not palatable to humans but loved by wildlife.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds
plants.notes: Bark historically used as a laxative. Good understory tree.
Riparian shrub/small tree. Good for erosion control along streams.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries highly attractive to birds
plants.notes: Early-blooming shrub with edible berries. Excellent wildlife plant.
Evergreen shrub with distinctive red bark. Drought tolerant.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds, thickets provide cover
plants.notes: Bright red stems provide winter color. Excellent riparian shrub.

plants.wildlife_value: Nuts eaten by birds and mammals
plants.notes: Native hazelnut produces edible nuts. Tolerates a range of conditions.
Uncommon native shrub with attractive fall color.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract pollinators, seeds eaten by birds
plants.notes: Drought tolerant once established. Cascading white flower clusters.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds
plants.notes: Native honeysuckle with distinctive paired black berries.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds, flowers attract pollinators
plants.notes: Oregon's state flower. Evergreen shrub with yellow flowers and blue berries.

plants.wildlife_value: Fruits eaten by birds, very early bloom for pollinators
plants.notes: One of the first shrubs to bloom in spring. Plum-like fruit.
Idaho's state flower. Fragrant white flowers.
Exfoliating bark. White flower clusters. Good riparian shrub.

plants.wildlife_value: Cherries eaten by many birds and mammals
plants.notes: Highly productive wildlife plant. Tart edible fruit.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds and mammals
plants.notes: Thorny native gooseberry with edible fruit.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds
plants.notes: Large native currant with blue-black berries. Pungent foliage.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract hummingbirds, berries eaten by birds
plants.notes: Showy spring-blooming shrub. Excellent hummingbird plant.

plants.wildlife_value: Rose hips eaten by birds and mammals, thickets provide nesting cover
plants.notes: Fragrant pink flowers. Thorny thickets provide excellent wildlife cover.

plants.wildlife_value: Rose hips eaten by birds
plants.notes: Native rose for wetter sites. Small pink clustered flowers.

plants.wildlife_value: Fruit eaten by birds and mammals
plants.notes: Large maple-like leaves. Thornless. Edible red raspberries.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by many birds, early flowers for hummingbirds
plants.notes: Very early spring bloomer. Salmon to red edible berries.
Riparian willow. Important for streambank stabilization.
Coastal and riparian willow.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries highly attractive to birds
plants.notes: Large native elderberry. Blue-black berries edible when cooked.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds
plants.notes: Red berries are toxic to humans. Excellent bird plant.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds and mammals
plants.notes: Evergreen shrub with edible black berries. Shade tolerant.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds and mammals
plants.notes: Bright red edible berries. Often grows on rotting logs in shade.
Native viburnum, uncommon in the Portland area.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds and mammals
plants.notes: Low-growing evergreen groundcover shrub. Excellent for dry slopes.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract pollinators
plants.notes: Nitrogen-fixing shrub. Fragrant white flower clusters.
Fragrant resinous leaves. Nitrogen-fixing. Good post-fire species.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds and mammals
plants.notes: Tough evergreen groundcover. Ubiquitous in Pacific Northwest forests.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract hummingbirds, berries eaten by birds
plants.notes: Native twining vine. Pink-purple flowers.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract hummingbirds
plants.notes: Native climbing vine with showy orange-red flowers.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds
plants.notes: Low-growing evergreen with yellow flowers and blue berries. Very shade tolerant.
Native gooseberry with showy flowers.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by many birds and mammals
plants.notes: Trailing native blackberry. Ancestor of many commercial blackberries.
Small native rose. Shade tolerant. Tiny pink flowers.
Compact native spirea with flat-topped white flower clusters.
Pink-flowered spirea. Good for moist or wet areas.

plants.wildlife_value: White berries eaten by birds in winter
plants.notes: Adaptable native shrub. Distinctive white berries persist into winter.
Low-growing trailing snowberry. Good groundcover for shady spots.
Delicate fan-shaped fronds on glossy black stems. Elegant shade fern.
Large, lacy deciduous fern. Very adaptable.
Evergreen fern with distinctive dimorphic fronds.
Large spreading fern for shady, moist locations.
Delicate small fern. Spreads by rhizomes to form colonies.
Often grows as an epiphyte on bigleaf maple trunks. Sweet-tasting rhizomes.
Most common fern in the Pacific Northwest. Evergreen and very tough.
Widespread deciduous fern. Can be aggressive in open areas.
Dense tufted sedge for wet areas.
Shade-tolerant woodland sedge.
Robust sedge for pond edges and marshy areas.
Wetland sedge. Good for rain gardens and pond margins.
Ornamental tufted grass with airy seed heads.
Cool-season native bunchgrass. Blue-green foliage.
Native bunchgrass for seasonally wet meadows.
Adaptable native rush. Spreads by rhizomes.
Classic wetland rush. Cylindrical green stems.
Flat-leaved native rush. Distinctive dark brown flower heads.
Blue-gray foliage. Good for rain gardens.
Short native bunchgrass for dry, open areas.
Woodland rush. Grass-like with drooping flower clusters.

plants.wildlife_value: Seeds eaten by waterfowl, provides nesting cover
plants.notes: Tall emergent bulrush for ponds and marshes.
Wetland bulrush. Good for pond margins.

plants.wildlife_value: Seeds and rhizomes eaten by waterfowl, nesting material
plants.notes: Iconic wetland plant. Can spread aggressively. All parts edible.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract many pollinators
plants.notes: Feathery foliage with flat-topped white flower clusters. Drought tolerant.
Native wild onion with pink flowers. Rocky dry slopes.
Native wild onion with drooping pink flower clusters.

plants.wildlife_value: Host plant for American Lady butterfly
plants.notes: Papery white everlasting flowers. Good for dried arrangements.

plants.wildlife_value: Nectar source for hummingbirds
plants.notes: Showy red and yellow flowers. Hummingbird magnet.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract pollinators
plants.notes: Late-season bloomer for moist areas. Lavender flowers.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract pollinators
plants.notes: Native aster with violet-blue flowers. Good for moist borders.
Shade-tolerant perennial for moist streambanks.
Native corm with white to pale blue flowers. Dry summer dormant.
Large blue flower spikes. Important historical food source for Indigenous peoples.
Brilliant blue flower meadows in spring. Edible bulb, important to Indigenous peoples.
Delicate nodding blue-purple bells. Tolerates dry, rocky soils.

plants.wildlife_value: Nectar source for many pollinators
plants.notes: Pioneer species after disturbance. Tall magenta flower spikes.
Showy annual with pink to lavender flowers. Blooms as the dry season begins.
Edible annual succulent. Round leaves surround the stem. Self-seeds readily.
Small spring ephemeral. Delicate white to pink flowers.
Tiny annual with blue and white two-lipped flowers.
Native annual with salmon-colored flowers. Dry open areas.
Native delphinium. Blue-purple flowers. Toxic if ingested.
Shade-tolerant native larkspur.
Native corm with dense clusters of purple flowers. Summer dormant.
Cyclamen-like flowers. Summer dormant. Dry woodland species.
Moist-site shooting star. Nodding pink flowers.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract native bees
plants.notes: Wooly silver foliage with bright yellow daisy flowers. Very drought tolerant.
Mottled leaves, nodding white flowers. Spring ephemeral.
Bright orange flowers. Self-seeds freely. Native to California but naturalized in Oregon.

plants.wildlife_value: Small fruits eaten by birds and mammals
plants.notes: Spreads by runners. Tiny sweet edible berries.

plants.wildlife_value: Fruits eaten by birds and mammals
plants.notes: Parent of commercial strawberry. Edible fruit.
Unusual mottled brown-purple pendant flowers. Spring ephemeral.

plants.wildlife_value: Excellent pollinator plant
plants.notes: Spherical heads of tiny blue flowers. Very attractive to bees.
Native terrestrial orchid with white-veined leaves. Dry to moist forest floors.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract many pollinators
plants.notes: Very large native perennial. Sap can cause skin blisters in sunlight.
Native coral bells. Good for rock gardens and woodland edges.
Shade-tolerant native heuchera for moist rocky areas.
Ground-covering perennial for moist shaded areas. White to pale lavender flowers.
Oregon's state wildflower. Purple to lavender blooms.

plants.wildlife_value: Nectar source for hummingbirds and butterflies
plants.notes: Showy orange nodding flowers with dark spots. Bulbs edible.
Delicate trailing evergreen. Paired pink bell-shaped flowers. Fragrant.
Delicate white star-shaped flowers. Spring ephemeral for dry meadows.

plants.wildlife_value: Host plant for several butterfly species
plants.notes: Nitrogen-fixing. Blue-purple flower spikes.

plants.wildlife_value: Host plant for butterflies
plants.notes: Large lupine with impressive blue-purple flower spikes. Nitrogen-fixing.
Nitrogen-fixing riparian lupine. Blue flowers.

plants.wildlife_value: Early pollen source for insects, leaves eaten by bears
plants.notes: Bold tropical appearance. Yellow spathe is among first spring blooms.
Glossy heart-shaped leaves. Good groundcover for moist shade.
Native mertensia with blue bell-shaped flowers.
Bright yellow snapdragon-like flowers. Loves wet seeps and streambanks.
Small yellow monkeyflower. Sticky foliage. Shade-tolerant.
Low groundcover for moist shaded areas. Tiny intricate green flowers.
Shade-tolerant penstemon relative. Rose-purple tubular flowers.

plants.wildlife_value: Red berries eaten by birds, important medicinal plant
plants.notes: Very spiny large-leaved shrub. Bright red berries. Important ethnobotanical plant.
Clover-like leaves fold at night. Excellent groundcover for deep shade.
Small wood sorrel with white flowers. Shade groundcover.
One of the earliest spring bloomers. Reddish-purple flowers.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract hummingbirds and bumblebees
plants.notes: Blue-purple penstemon. One of the more adaptable native penstemons.
Deeply cut leaves. Lavender-pink flowers. Cliff and rocky slope species.
Deep blue-purple flowers. Good for moist garden borders.
Flowers before leaves appear. Can spread aggressively by rhizomes.
Cream-colored flowers. Sticky glandular stems.
Yellow flowers. Palmate leaves. Open meadows and dry slopes.
Woodland perennial with drooping cream bells. Red berries in fall.
Woodland lily relative with cream-white drooping bells.
Low spreading perennial. Purple flower heads. Historically used medicinally.

plants.wildlife_value: Tubers eaten by waterfowl, important food source
plants.notes: Arrowhead-shaped leaves. Edible tubers important to Indigenous peoples. Aquatic/emergent.
Trailing aromatic groundcover. Minty fragrance. Tiny white flowers.
Rosette of leaves with white flower clusters. Rocky outcrops.
Larger saxifrage for wet meadows and streambanks.
Succulent native sedum. Excellent for green roofs and rock gardens.
Rosette-forming native sedum with yellow flowers. Excellent for rock walls.
Tall native mallow with white to pink flowers. Native to Willamette Valley prairies.
Threatened species. Pink flowers. Important for native bee conservation.
Iris family, grass-like leaves with small blue flowers. Good for rain gardens.

plants.wildlife_value: Excellent late-season pollinator plant
plants.notes: Important late-season nectar source. Can spread aggressively.
Bold large-leaved perennial. Very toxic. Wet meadows and streambanks.

plants.wildlife_value: Flowers attract many pollinators
plants.notes: Blue-purple flower spikes. Good for moist to wet areas.
Aquatic/semi-aquatic perennial. Small blue flowers. Good for pond margins.

plants.wildlife_value: Host plant for several fritillary butterfly species
plants.notes: Small blue-purple violet. Important butterfly larval host.
Bright yellow violet. Lovely groundcover for moist shaded areas.
Trailing evergreen violet. Yellow flowers with dark veins.
Low trailing shrublet with white flower clusters. Dry rocky slopes.
Distinctive three-part leaves smell like vanilla when dried. White flower spikes.
White-flowered woodland anemone. Elegant spring ephemeral.
Glossy heart-shaped leaves. Excellent groundcover for shaded areas. Spicy ginger scent.
Delicate native orchid. Very difficult to transplant. Depends on specific fungal associates.
Large shade perennial with pink spurred flowers. Spreads by rhizomes.
Delicate ferny foliage with pink heart-shaped flowers. Long bloom season.

plants.wildlife_value: Red berries eaten by birds
plants.notes: Low groundcover in the dogwood family. White flower bracts. Red berries.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds
plants.notes: Elegant arching stems with terminal white flower clusters. Red berries.
Starry white flowers along stem. Striped green berries turn red.
Zigzag stems with dangling bell-shaped flowers. Red berries.
Very early spring bloomer. Small purple-blue flowers above round leaves.
Fringe-petaled white to pink flowers on tall stalks. Good shade groundcover.
Delicate divided foliage. Purple stamens. Elegant woodland perennial.
Frothy white flower spikes. Excellent groundcover for moist shade.
Single-leaved variety of foam flower. Tolerates drier shade conditions.
Produces plantlets at leaf bases. Popular houseplant. Native to Pacific Northwest forests.
Tiny star-shaped pink flowers above a whorl of leaves.
Large mottled leaves with sessile dark red flowers. Spring ephemeral.
Classic white trillium. Flowers age to pink/purple. Slow-growing from seed.
Unusual recurved white petals. Delicate groundcover for dry to moist shade.

plants.wildlife_value: Berries eaten by birds
plants.notes: White flower clusters. Shiny red (or white) berries. TOXIC to humans.
Native cress for moist shaded areas. White flowers.
Small delicate shade perennial. Tiny white flowers. Sticky seeds.
Pink-veined white flowers. Self-seeds readily. Edible leaves.
Biennial that invades forest understories. Produces allelopathic chemicals that inhibit native plant growth and mycorrhizal fungi. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Aggressive colonizer of dry open areas and disturbed ground. Releases allelopathic compounds. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Vigorous woody climbing vine that smothers trees and shrubs. Fluffy seed heads disperse widely. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Nitrogen-fixing shrub that alters soil chemistry and outcompetes native plants. Seed bank persists for decades. Requires multi-year removal strategy.

plants.health_hazard: All parts toxic; sap causes skin and eye irritation
plants.notes: Evergreen understory shrub that invades forests. All parts are highly toxic. Berries spread by birds. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Smothers ground layer and climbs trees, increasing their vulnerability to wind throw. All cultivars invasive. Requires multi-year removal strategy.

plants.health_hazard: Sap causes severe photodermatitis — painful blisters and potential permanent scarring or blindness on contact with sunlight. Do not touch without full protective gear.
plants.notes: Extremely large invasive forb. State-listed noxious weed. Requires multi-year removal strategy. Contact authorities before attempting removal.
Spreads aggressively by stolons and wind-dispersed seeds. Forms dense mats that exclude native plants. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Annual that produces thousands of seeds, explosively ejected up to 20 feet. Invades riparian areas. Requires multi-year removal strategy.

plants.health_hazard: All parts toxic; rhizome sap causes skin irritation
plants.notes: Invades wetlands, ponds, and riparian corridors. Dense rhizome mats displace native aquatic vegetation. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Spreading groundcover that forms dense mats smothering native understory plants. Commonly escapes from gardens. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
State-listed noxious weed. Invades and dominates wetlands, eliminating native plant diversity. A single plant can produce over 2 million seeds per year. Requires multi-year removal strategy.

plants.health_hazard: All parts toxic, especially roots and unripe berries. Can cause severe poisoning.
plants.notes: Large perennial with dark berries spread by birds. Not native to the Pacific Northwest. Toxic to humans and livestock. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Includes Japanese, Giant, and Bohemian knotweed. Extremely aggressive; rhizomes can penetrate concrete and re-sprout from tiny fragments. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Most common and widespread invasive shrub in Portland. Dense thorny canes outcompete native vegetation. Fruits edible but spread aggressively by birds. Requires multi-year removal strategy.

plants.health_hazard: All parts toxic; causes skin irritation
plants.notes: Spreading tuberous perennial invading forest understories. Bright orange-red berries spread by birds. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Shade-tolerant grass invading forest understories. Rapidly displaces native ground layer vegetation. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Despite attracting adult butterflies, provides no larval host value. Colonizes riparian areas and disturbed ground. Sterile varieties still not recommended. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Twining vine with extensive root system. Smothers native shrubs and groundcovers. Very difficult to eradicate. Requires multi-year removal strategy.

plants.health_hazard: Sharp leaf edges can cause cuts
plants.notes: Large clumping grass producing millions of wind-dispersed seeds. Forms dense monocultures. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Annual invading forest understories and disturbed areas. Explosive seed dispersal. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Annual / biennial with pungent odor. Invades forest understories. Spreads rapidly by explosive seed dispersal. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Very large-leaved perennial spreading by rhizomes. Invades riparian areas. Easily confused with native coltsfoot. Requires multi-year removal strategy.

plants.health_hazard: All parts toxic when fresh; sap may irritate skin
plants.notes: Spring ephemeral forming dense carpets that outcompete native spring wildflowers. Spreads by bulbils and tubers. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Dense canopy shades out native understory plants. Seeds prolifically and self-seeds in forests. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Extremely fast-growing and prolific seeder. Produces allelopathic chemicals. Cutting stimulates suckering. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Invasive hawthorn spreading into natural areas from ornamental plantings. Berries spread by birds. Thorny thickets displace native shrubs. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Evergreen tree invading Portland-area forests. Dense shade excludes native understory. Berries spread widely by birds. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Nitrogen-fixing tree that alters soil chemistry. Spreads aggressively by root sprouts. Cutting stimulates suckering. Requires multi-year removal strategy.
Tall aromatic perennial escaping from gardens. Produces abundant seeds. Colonizes roadsides and disturbed areas.
Low creeping perennial spreading by stolons. Invades moist areas and wetland margins, smothering native groundcovers.
Highly aggressive wetland grass forming dense monocultures that eliminate native wetland vegetation. One of the most problematic invasives in Portland wetlands.

plants.health_hazard: Leaves and seeds contain cyanogenic compounds
plants.notes: Dense evergreen hedge plant escaping into natural areas. Berries spread by birds. Forms shade-tolerant understory layer that prevents native regeneration.
Trailing evergreen groundcover forming impenetrable mats that exclude all native vegetation. Very common in Portland gardens and natural areas. Extremely difficult to eradicate.
Mature specimens cast extremely dense shade, preventing all native understory growth. Prolific seeder. Look-alike to native bigleaf maple but with milky sap in leaf petioles.
Mature tree producing hundreds of thousands of winged seeds annually. Root system sends up persistent suckers after cutting. Allelopathic to surrounding vegetation.
Large mature specimens seed prolifically into surrounding natural areas. Thorny branches make removal hazardous. Berries highly attractive to birds, accelerating spread.
Mature trees produce large quantities of berries spread by birds across the landscape. Dense evergreen canopy eliminates native understory regeneration.
Mature trees fix nitrogen and alter soil chemistry, enabling further invasion. Extremely persistent root system. Stump treatment essential after cutting to prevent suckering.
